sentences.text: OK.
[SIGHS] When I came to concentration camp-- I-- I am going back-- I went to a school.
My parents thought that--
No.
Before I went to a German school.
And I knew how to speak a very good German, because the city I was born, there were a lot of German people.
So then when I came to concentration camp, I spoke a very good German, that the Germans could not believe that I'm not born in Germany.
So they picked me to be a interpreter.
They came--
Yes.
I was born.
This city was 90% German, in Poland.
Yes.
This was in addition to.
Yes.
Yes.
Anyway, I had a lot of German neighbors.
And I spoke a very, very good German.
When we came to concentration camp, the German could-- the German people which worked in the camp-- the leaders-- they could not believe.
And they said, what did they do with you?
You're such intelligent girls.
And, look, we had-- They were on the walls, they had everywhere, papers.
Don't speak to the girls.
These are girls.
They came from prison.
They are prostitutes.
And they said, I can't believe.
Even some Germans could not believe what they had done to us.
Anyway, I spoke a perfect German.
So they asked me, [GERMAN].
And they took me to be interpreter.
So I was an interpreter, and got a little bit better food in concentration camp.
Maybe I did this for about five, six weeks.
Different girls came from Hungary, and different countries.
And they didn't know one word German.
So this what I did with my sister.
We both spoke a very good German.
A very, very good German.
I still speak a very good German now.
[SIGHS]
I was very intelligent.
So they picked me in concentration camp to do the hats of the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a very precise work.
You have to go here, four years to engineering school to do it.
But I caught this right away, and I did it.
And I was working.
And you had to be very, very precise.
If you were not, with a pin, you could have your hands cut off, working on the machine without food for hours and hours.
We were gone to work every single day maybe five miles in the winter, in the snow.
And whatever we could steal, we survived on it.
A little bit of soup.
A piece of-- a drop of apple from the floor.
Whatever we could find.
Whenever you were caught to pick up something, you were beaten to death.
My sister was caught in concentration camp to pick up a piece of bread from the floor.
They rounded the whole camp.
[SIGHS] She was only 15.
They round up.
And they beat her so terribly.
And everybody had to look.
That I thought-- and they cut her hair off.
This was maybe a few months after we had already hair.
They cut our hair flat in Auschwitz.
[SIGHS] I just got so sick looking.
But somehow you survive.
We were so afraid to talk to anybody.
We were afraid to look at anybody.
We were punished for every little thing.
They made us like animals.
We couldn't speak.
We couldn't see anybody.
The people at concentration camp, in the factory they were working a lot of different nationalities.
There were Italians.
They were German war prisoners which escaped.
They were French, and Americans.
They said to us, we can't understand.
What have they done to you?
You're so intelligent.
[SIGHS] So they all helped us in concentration camp.
We were very lucky.
The Italian brought us soup.
The French brought us whatever.
We were in concentration camp, only the Jews.
But all the other nationalities were in working camps.
They could free go out to the village and come.
We were just always, we were under barbed wires.
We could not, and always watched.
Every few yards was a SS.
They were not prisoners.
They were working people.
They were restricted.
But they got-- they had better than we did-- we had.
They were Italian war prisoners.
And they were French.
And they were from all over Europe.
This was 1944.
But they worked.
They had to work.
But they could move freely.
So they helped us.
God forbid if we caught a piece of food.
They gave us food secretly in the factory where we worked.
I worked in the ammunition factory.
If they caught us, we were beaten, and punished, and grounded-- not getting food for days and days.
We walked every day about two, three miles to the factory to work, because our barracks were maybe two miles away.
We walked in wooden shoes.
It was winter snow, and when you walk on wood, snow gets glued onto the sole.
So some was-- some was high, and some was low.
And I can't even remember.
We lived without clothes.
We didn't have any clothes.
And this is how we lived.
We never even had the chance to watch ourself.
I guess if you're young, you survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't believe this.
This was seven days a week.
Then we came home to a barrack.
It was so cold.
[SIGHS] We paired up.
And when the Germans did not see, we paired up in our [NON-ENGLISH] to warm our bodies.
[DRINKS] One day I recall walking to work.
My sister picked up a piece of bread, and they caught her on the floor.
And they caught her.
And they were beating her so terribly.
This was maybe two months before liberation.
And they cut her hair.
They round up the whole camp.
It was just terrible.
She was so beat, I thought she would never, never make it.
Just for picking a piece of bread-- a piece of old, stale bread.
[SOBBING] This how we survived.
All the other nationalities, secretly they helped us.
They gave us whatever they could.
I remember one Italian young man, he gave me his portion.
They were free to walk to the village, and he was caught, and he was grounded for a month.
We found this out later, after liberation.
Well, [SIGHS] [SOBBING] we were in-- we were going every single day to work.
We lived in barracks.
We were about 250 girls with terrible, terrible conditions.
There was no food.
Every morning we were grounded.
Every morning they had appeals, cold, freezing.
[SIGHS] And they lined us up to go to the factories.
So we marched to the factory.
The factory was on the border of Czech and Germany.
The name of the factory was Bad Kudowa Saxon.
It was an underground ammunition factory which you could never see anything on the top.
The roof looked like a forest.
And we worked in the factory.
They were working every nationality.
There were Russian prisoners.
There were German war-- how you call it, escapees from the front.
They were French.
They were Italian.
They were Czechs.
Anyway, we went day after day to work.
The factory looked like a mountain, because it was a German-built factory in the Czechs mountains, and they manufactured ammunition.
Yes.
And I was working.
I was quite intelligent.
So I made the head of the [NON-ENGLISH].
I was on the very precise [BACKROUND NOISE] machine.
I was in concentration camp exactly nine months.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, can I bring something back, what I saw in Auschwitz?
We were so confused in Auschwitz, because everything was going so fast.
We stayed in the barracks for about three days, and all of a sudden a lady start to scream, a young girl.
Now I recall it, she came pregnant, in her ninth month to Auschwitz.
I mentioned this.
She delivered the baby.
And they took her baby aways.
The SS wrapped her in the newspaper.
I don't know what happened to that baby.
Yes.
[SIGHS] This was a terrible incident.
One day, being in Auschwitz, I had to go into the bathroom.
We were so-- they deprive us-- they deprived us of all humanity.
They didn't let us do anything.
So somehow I was wandering around in Auschwitz, and I came in to a lab.
And the lab, they were drawing blood from the people for experiments-- for twins.
And I could see drawn the blood so long, till the heads dropped.
There were a lot of other incidents like this.
How did you  manage to get around?
Somehow I-- they didn't see me.
It was surviving Auschwitz, and surviving the war was just plain luck.
I was just lucky.
It was nothing I could do about it.
I was just plain lucky.
At times, even Germans helped us in the factory, because they just could not believe that we were so intelligent.
In the factory in concentration camp in Czechoslovakia, they had big signs on the walls-- don't speak to the girls because these are criminals.
They're escapees.
They're prostitutes.
When the Germans saw we start to work at the machines, they were like-- they were machines like you had to be a 6th year engineer to do this.
Very, very precise work.
We did the hats of the [NON-ENGLISH].
So they said, I can't believe.
What have you done with us at times?
Can you think of any other incidents?
You've just recalled a few, any more incidents that you recall from Auschwitz or any of the other--
When I came into Auschwitz, I was so confused.
People were dying [SIGHS] at the barbed wires.
[SIGHS] People were screaming.
[SIGHS]
One lady in Auschwitz, I'm sure she was in her ninth month at the wire.
She was screaming for her husband.
She said, where are you?
I love you.
Where are you?
She was bleeding to death and collapsing.
Every minute people were dying in the barracks.
They took them out every minute for three days.
After three days in Auschwitz, [SIGHS] merchant came from Czechoslovakia.
And he picked 250 girls.
We were going through selections like every day.
The healthiest were picked to work.
What they thought they were not so healthy, they were picked to crematorium.
After we were building, we were every day we walked back to our camp.
It was a camp with barbed wire.
We were in that camp for about nine months.
[SIGHS] And every day people were dying.
Sick people.
They got sicker and sicker.
There was no way of medication.
There was no way of hospitals.
Whoever was strong, they survived.
My sister, she was quite sick.
She got-- she was bleeding from her lungs.
So I sort of got a hold of some medication, and I shared the food with her.
We got a very small ration.
Like a little bit of black oil and a piece of bread.
Whatever I could share I gave her.
She should recover.
I don't-- [SIGHS] we got it some kind underground in the factory, the medication.
It was everything underground.
I think the Italians helped us.
My sister was about 15 at the time.
When did it come to an end?
We were in this-- we were in concentration camp in Bad Kudowa in Saxon for nine months.
After nine months, planes were flying over the camp dropping leaflets.
And they said, the war is over.
And we sort of saw that it's a panic.
That the SS is running away.
So the Czechs partisans, and the Russian which worked with us in the concentration camp, they were war prisoners.
They opened the camps, and they said, you are free now.
We were so confused.
And we say, what's free?
Where do we go?
Where do we belong?
So everybody in Czechoslovakia, the people from the border towns took-- each family took in one or two people until we got on our feet.
Back to when the leaflets dropped.
Oh, when the leaflets dropped, they said that the war is over.
They run away.
They ran away.
No.
It was overnight.
They ran away.
They-- they were guarding the camps, and we saw them running away.
This was in Czechoslovakia.
This was in Bad Kudowa.
And then the Czech partisan and the Russian partisan came, and they opened the camps.
And they said, you don't have to worry now.
You are free.
And we saw the Germans.
They were lined up-- no, it was different.
Once the camps-- once the war was over, the partisans-- the-- the Czechs and the Russians who liberated us, they gave us a password.
They opened the camp, it was still going on, war.
They were fighting from house to house in Bad Kudowa.
And they were hiding us.
The soldiers were hiding us in basements.
And they said, if they catch you, if they're Russians you have a password.
So we had a password to say.
The password was [?
Praha ?] [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, [?
Praha ?] is beautiful.
This was our password.
So we-- we were-- every time the Nazis, the Germans-- this was in the mountains of the border Germany and , Czechoslovakia.
They were fighting in the mountains to the last minute.
They would not surrender.
So the-- the Russians and the-- and the Czechs were hiding us in private homes till everything quiet down.
So you came out of this camp.
And you were taken to a home?
We were taken to a home.
But the war was still-- the Nazis would not surrender.
They were still shooting from the mountains.
It was a mountain country.
The Czech people were very good people.
Thanks to the Czech people, we survived.
I remember incidents when we worked in concentration camp, we worked in Czechoslovakia, the peasant women came out, and they cooked for us food.
And they brought food for us.
It was almost at the end.
It was 1944, the end of the war.
The Germans could not get any food.
[INAUDIBLE] they even took our food, and they [SIGHS] they were sharing with us.
The Czech people were so wonderful.
We worked at the end.
We worked after the concentration camp, after this-- the Czechs took over, we worked-- and they made us carry stones with no aim.
We were going forth and back-- a hundred pound stone.
We were so skinny at the time.
We were so sick and so tired.
So the Czech people, when the train left, when the train was going through where we were working, they threw their lunches down.
It was flying from the windows like confetti.
The German SS gathered all the food, and they divided this between us.
And this was sort of at the end.
So this was after you worked in the factory, you actually were carrying stones.
They kept you busy that way.
Well, first I was working in the factory, but then it was after-- after the Russian came in, they took us further into Germany, where they did not occupy.
And they made us carry-- they made us do something just to punish.
So made us carry stones.
Walk.
Everything was walking.
It was a march.
It was a death march.
People were dropping dead.
Was maybe for two weeks a death march.
People were dropping dead every single minute.
No place.
In barracks, wherever we could find.
They put us up at night in barracks.
And they put us up at night in the street, in the mountains.
It's-- I can't even remember.
For the first time in my life, I saw that milk was white.
I haven't seen milk for six years.
I haven't seen meat for six weeks-- for six years.
We were in the houses.
Every Czech person took in a girl.
They took in my sister, and they took me in.
They fed us.
They dressed us.
And after a while, they start to form DP camps in Czechoslovakia.
Then-- [SIGHS]
After the war, yes.
With very few people we could.
We knew that few survived.
My girlfriend, Bella, survived.
We met from the ghetto.
We stuck together-- maybe 10, 12 people.
Then the war was over.
I got a little bit on my feet.
Then [SIGHS] I was trying to search for my family who survived.
At this point I was in Czechoslovakia.
But with my sister.
Always with my sister.
At times, my sister was so tired and so sick that she said she does not want to live anymore.
She couldn't walk.
I weighed like 90 pounds, and I'm 5'5'.
So I pulled my sister by the hair, and I said, wherever I'm going, you are going.
You're not going to give up now.
[SOBS]
At that time I was in Czechoslovakia and [SIGHS] the war was over.
This was 1945, May the 8th.
So now, [SOBS] we said, where are we going?
We knew everybody was dead.
There was no place for us to go.
I did not to want to go back to Poland.
Poland was terrible to us.
They were traitors everywhere.
I'm going back.
I went back to Poland in 1939 to get some clothes.
I smuggled on a train from Lodz to my city, Konstantynow.
And this was maybe a seven mile.
So they said to me, right away there were people what I went to school with them, and they said, what?
You are alive?
You're not going to be alive much longer.
So I jumped on the train, jumping-- I was young; I was 15-- and went back to the ghetto.
Going back [SIGHS] now, after the war-- Did you know where-- anything about your brother?
Yes.
I went back, I didn't know.
At this point, this was six years later.
I didn't know anything about my brother.
I was, at that time I was liberated.
A little while later, the American joint came in, and they started to form DP camps.
So I signed up, and I went to-- to a DP camp near Munich.
And right away, I said, now I'm going to search for my family.
So my sister and I, we jumped on a train.
This was after the war.
And we went into Poland.
No.
We just-- no money.
Everything was still-- we just jumped on the train with no food, with nothing.
Well, I have to go back before I jumped on the train.
In German-- when I was liberated, I met-- no, I jumped on the train, and I went back to Poland.
I don't know what I had.
I probably had nothing.
Whatever we had, whatever we wore.
[SIGHS] And I-- And I went to the Jewish community in Lodz.
And I registered myself.
And I said, send out letters.
I knew that my brother was in Russia.
I sent out letters to Leningrad, Stalingrad, Moscow, and middle Asia.
My brother, the last letter we had from him, he said that he's-- he was sent to Siberia.
Because he registered to come back home.
Conditions were so bad, and he was young.
He was 17 years old.
Well, anyway, I sent, I thought I'm going to send letters to every major city in hope that my brother survived.
So one of the letters arrived to Siberia in the camp he was.
And everybody said, it's impossible that your sister survived.
So my brother, who lives now in New York, he said, if my sister survived, [SIGHS] that is a miracle.
Everybody said that probably they were kapos, they were killers, because we had a lot of bad people in ghetto.
So I said-- my brother said, I know where I'm coming from.
If my sister survived, this was plain luck.
He jumped on a train and he hitchhiked to Poland.
Took about-- this was 1945 in December.
And I stayed [SIGHS] in Poland.
I stayed in Lodz for about a few days hoping.
I didn't know that he survived, that he's coming back.
Anyway, I couldn't stay so long in Poland.
Somehow I went back to Czechoslovakia.
And my sister stayed in Lodz with my friend, Bella, and Bella's sister.
We all paired up, whoever survived.
And one date, December, New Year's, I stayed-- I met some people in Dachau after the war.
Which we survived the concentration camp.
We just, we're going with no aim.
We didn't know where we were going.
So I met some friends in Dachau.
And she had a brother which survived in Dachau.
And he had already a home, take me away from a German.
So we had a roof over our head.
We didn't know what-- where we were going.
Anyway, I-- [DRINKS] New Year's Eve I was-- I was in Czechoslovakia.
New Year's Eve, I was hoping that I'm going to meet somebody from my family.
So from Bad Kudowa, from Czechoslovakia, I went to Regensburg, which is a border town from Germany, you know, Germany to Czechoslovakia.
And I was hoping that maybe, maybe this is the night I'm going to reunite with my family.
Went to Regensburg.
And everywhere they formed DP camps.
So I went to the DP camp.
We were young.
This was already January, we were liberated in May.
And I met some people from my hometown, from Lodz.
So I met a friend, and he said to me, you know what?
It's New Year's Eve.
And let's go to a dance.
I say, go to a dance?
I'm still was wearing the striped clothes with the wooden shoes.
So he said--
